title: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)
summary: The repository artifact is a wrapper/version of University of Michigan CME item OwlJ, a Jesus College MS 29 encoding whose 1,794 poetic line strings match the current OTA 1684 OwlJ component and current MEDictionary raw XML. All identify J. W. H. Atkins's edition as their textual source, but the OTA statement combines a 1922 date with New York publisher Russell & Russell, strongly suggesting the 1971 reprint carrier while U-M cites the underlying Cambridge 1922 edition. Atkins independently transcribed the Jesus and Cotton witnesses on facing pages. Current scholarship reconstructs both manuscripts as independent descendants of a lost common exemplar X; Cotton is therefore a collateral witness, not an ancestor of OwlJ. Complete current facsimiles of both physical codices are publicly viewable and cached locally with exact work portions.
review_notes: The cataloged text is the complete Jesus-witness version; the abstract poem, Cotton version, editions, encodings, repository artifact, and image objects remain distinct graph layers.
Current manuscript foliation is used for direct citation. Atkins, OTA, and CME legacy foliation is crosswalked by subtracting 73: old 229r–241v equals current 156r–168v.

The repository BODY has 47 folio MILESTONE elements plus five locator strings embedded literally in L text. The same five labels are unstructured in current OTA SGML and current MEDictionary XML; the 1922 scan shows them as marginal edition navigation, not verse.
The cached Cambridge 1922 scan is a complete public textual surrogate and comparison source, not proof that it was the exact physical carrier keyed by OTA.

Rights statements are scoped to the named entity and component. Medieval text, modern edition, digital encoding, manuscript image, catalog data, and repository file rights do not inherit automatically across relations.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for OwlJ
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The pinned file has 18 DIV1/@NODE attributes and 75 EPB elements absent from the May 2026 MEDictionary XML, while its 1,794 poetic line strings and five literal locator labels remain the same.
The submodule is a distinct repository and is not edited by this manifest change.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item OwlJ
entities.description: The current public U-M web text of the Jesus College MS 29 version, with a bibliographic header identifying Atkins 1922 and an explicit current public-domain statement.
notes: U-M promotes the opening Latin rubric to HEAD and exposes 1,794 poem L nodes.
Its header's 1190–1210 CREATION date is retained as historical catalog metadata, not accepted as settled current dating.

entities.label: MEDictionary current raw CME OwlJ XML, May 2026 reformatted release
entities.description: The current individually downloadable raw XML distributed by the Middle English Compendium site after the corpus-wide May 2026 reformatting.
notes: The BODY contains 1,794 L, 19 HEAD, 18 supplied DIV1, 75 PB, 47 MILESTONE, and the same five literal folio labels as OTA and the pinned repository file.
The download page warns that inconsistencies remain despite the common-schema reformatting.

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive item 1684, OwlJ Jesus-witness component
entities.description: The current OTA plain-text/SGML component for MS J within a two-component deposited item that also supplies OwlC for the Cotton witness.
notes: The SGML has 1,795 L tags because it serializes the Latin rubric as an initial L; the following 1,794 L tags are poem lines.
It has 47 locdoc/div0 containers, while the same five edition folio labels later left literal in U-M/CME are already embedded in line text.

entities.label: Oxford Text Archive item 1684, OwlC Cotton-witness component
entities.description: The collateral Cotton Caligula A IX component distributed in the same OTA item as OwlJ.
notes: This is supporting comparison material. It is not upstream of the Jesus-witness OwlJ text.

entities.label: Atkins 1922 Cambridge edition
entities.description: J. W. H. Atkins's edition, printing independently examined Cotton and Jesus texts on facing pages with introduction, notes, translation, and glossary.

bibliographic.citation: J. W. H. Atkins, ed., The Owl and the Nightingale (Cambridge: University Press, 1922), xc + 231 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Owl and the Nightingale

notes: Jesus text J occupies odd printed pp. 3–151; Cotton text C occupies even pp. 2–150.
The separately modeled Internet Archive scan is a textual surrogate and comparison source. It is not established as the exact carrier used during electronic keying.
John Stevenson's 1838 Roxburghe Club edition predates Atkins and was based principally on Cotton with some Jesus readings; Atkins is not described as the poem's first publication.

entities.label: Atkins 1971 Russell & Russell reprint
entities.description: The New York reprint carrier whose bibliographic profile resolves the OTA statement 'Atkins 1922 edition, NY: Russell and Russell.'

bibliographic.citation: J. W. H. Atkins, ed., The Owl and the Nightingale (New York: Russell & Russell, 1971), xc + 231 pp.; reprint of the 1922 Cambridge edition.
bibliographic.title: The Owl and the Nightingale

notes: Library of Congress fixed field 008 records r19711922 and the notes identify facing Cotton/Jesus texts and first publication in 1922.
OTA does not explicitly say 1971, so exact carrier dependence remains a supported inference rather than a confirmed production fact.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Atkins 1922
entities.description: Complete digital reproduction of the 1922 Cambridge edition, including front matter, facing Cotton and Jesus texts, notes, translation, glossary, and appendices.

notes: The complete edition is reproduced; the two printed manuscript plates are not a complete manuscript facsimile.

entities.reproduction_format: PDF, OCR text, page-number map, and Internet Archive metadata

entities.label: Internet Archive controlled-borrow scan of Atkins 1971
entities.description: An extant digital reproduction of the Russell & Russell carrier, exposed through Internet Archive's print-disabled lending collection rather than as an unrestricted download.
entities.reproduction_format: LCP-encrypted PDF/EPUB and lending viewer; public metadata only cached

entities.label: Oxford, Jesus College MS 29, Part II
entities.description: The trilingual poetic-anthology component of a composite codex and the manuscript witness transcribed for OwlJ.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian describes Part I as Polychronicon, ff. 1r–143bv, and Part II as the trilingual anthology, ff. 144r–257v.

physical_description.extent: Composite codex: ff. ii + 144 + 114 + ii; poem at current ff. 156ra–168vb
physical_description.format: Two-column manuscript text in Part II
notes: The complete current digital object has 532 canvases.
Current f. 156r retains the older pencil folio number 229, establishing the legacy/current crosswalk.
The Jesus scribe adapted and modernized the shared text more freely than the Cotton scribe.

entities.label: Reconstructed lost common exemplar X
entities.description: The lost common textual exemplar reconstructed from physical and textual correspondences between Jesus and Cotton. It is a transmission hypothesis, not the authorial original and not an extant cataloged manuscript.
notes: Current scholarship supports independent copying of both surviving manuscripts from X.
No shelfmark, holding repository, public reproduction, or secure copying date exists for this reconstructed node.

entities.label: Reported lost manuscript of ‘De conflictu inter philomenam et bubonem in anglicis’
entities.description: A separately reported lost manuscript that Ker 1963 considered likely to have contained The Owl and the Nightingale. The identification is preserved as a qualified report, not as an extant witness or as the reconstructed common exemplar X.
notes: No shelfmark, holding repository, facsimile, transcription, or secure relation to X was established from the consulted public source.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Caligula A IX
entities.description: The collateral complete witness of The Owl and the Nightingale, transmitted at ff. 233r–246r in a composite Middle English and Anglo-Norman codex.

date_statements.label: composite manuscript dating
date_statements.value: 1250–1299; likely post-1272 for the poem witness

physical_description.extent: 261 folios plus endleaves and flyleaves; poem at ff. 233r–246r
physical_description.format: Two columns; DIMEV notes the poem is written as prose
notes: The complete current IIIF object has 543 canvases.
Canvas 501 / f. 246r contains the poem's end and the beginning of the following item.
The BL catalog's more precise composition-origin suggestion for the poem is kept attributed to that record and is not promoted here to certain work geography.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of Jesus College MS 29
entities.description: A 532-canvas complete digital object for the physical composite codex, cached locally at a practical non-upscaling 1800-pixel profile.
notes: The Owl and the Nightingale occupies current ff. 156r–168v, IIIF canvases 319–344 of 532.
The manifest range LOG_0006 deep-links the work.

entities.label: British Library complete IIIF facsimile of Cotton MS Caligula A IX
entities.description: A 543-canvas complete digital object for the physical codex, cached locally at a practical 1200-pixel access profile.
notes: The poem occupies ff. 233r–246r, IIIF canvases 475–501 of 543.

entities.label: Fein 2022 Jesus-based edition and translation
entities.description: Susanna Fein's modern Jesus-witness edition and facing translation, with a comprehensive introduction and conservative correction through comparison with Cotton.

bibliographic.citation: Susanna Fein, ed. and trans., The Owl and the Nightingale and the English Poems of Oxford, Jesus College, MS 29 (II) (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2022; digital Middle English Texts Series edition, 2024).
bibliographic.title: The Owl and the Nightingale and the English Poems of Oxford, Jesus College, MS 29 (II)

notes: The edited poem and translation occupy PDF pp. 75–157; the article-style introduction begins at PDF p. 320 and the edition note at p. 322.
This later edition is an authoritative supporting source but not the source of the pinned OwlJ encoding.

entities.label: Ker 1963 EETS facsimile of the surviving manuscripts
entities.description: The published facsimile reproducing the surviving Jesus College 29 and British Museum Cotton Caligula A IX witnesses, with an introduction by N. R. Ker.

bibliographic.citation: N. R. Ker, intro., The Owl and the Nightingale: Reproduced in Facsimile from the Surviving Manuscripts, Jesus College Oxford 29 and British Museum Cotton Caligula A.ix, EETS Original Series 251 (Oxford: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society, 1963).
bibliographic.title: The Owl and the Nightingale: Reproduced in Facsimile from the Surviving Manuscripts
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 251
bibliographic.publisher: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society

notes: No complete public digital copy of this edition was established during the 15 July 2026 review; current complete manuscript image objects are available separately from the holding institutions.

entities.label: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 record 2307
entities.description: The current official DIMEV prerelease catalog record for the poem and its two witnesses.
notes: The dataset records 1,794 lines in couplets, two witnesses, DIMEV 2307, NIMEV 1384, and subjects debates, birds, animals, and birds as narrators.
The version is explicitly prerelease software/data; only fields observed in the cached version are asserted here.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 credits Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston.

rights.basis: The Kyvero-Press/CME repository root declares the Unlicense for software, source, and binary forms, but its scope over inherited corpus XML data is not explicit; the file itself also retains a legacy OTA personal-scholarly-use notice.
notes: Do not silently replace this artifact-level ambiguity with the clearer current U-M item statement or the OTA deposit license.

rights.component: Current U-M digital item and web text
rights.basis: The current item states that the work has been identified as public domain and free of known copyright restrictions, and exposes the RightsStatements.org NoC-US label.

The official download page says raw CME files are available for free downloading with no restrictions on use or reuse, and the file's own current availability paragraph identifies the work as public domain and permits commercial reuse without permission.
The current OTA landing page assigns Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 to the deposited digital item.
notes: This license applies to the OTA digital resource and does not flow upstream to Atkins, the manuscripts, or the medieval work.

rights.basis: The current OTA landing page assigns Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 to the deposited digital item.

rights.component: Atkins's 1922 published edition, separate from the modern Internet Archive scan and delivery layer
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1922. Internet Archive's exact item metadata marks possible-copyright-status as NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for region US and records the scanning review's finding of no visible copyright notice; current Copyright Office duration guidance independently places U.S.-published works from 1922 in the public domain.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and delivery metadata, separate from the underlying 1922 edition
rights.jurisdiction: United States and provider delivery terms
rights.basis: The provider's item metadata reports possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for region US and makes the derivatives publicly downloadable, but it supplies no separate open license for the modern scan/delivery layer. This qualified record therefore does not convert the provider flag into an unscoped worldwide public-domain claim.
The reprint was issued in 1971. The underlying 1922 edition is public domain in the United States, but no exact 1971 rights notice, renewal status, or newly contributed material was inspected.
notes: The catalog metadata is reusable as factual evidence; this record does not authorize reproducing the exact 1971 carrier as such.

rights.component: Internet Archive controlled-borrow scan and delivery layer, separate from the underlying 1971 carrier
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The metadata exposes the scan only in print-disabled/in-library collections; unrestricted PDF, OCR, and page-image derivatives are private and available derivatives are LCP-encrypted. No item-specific reuse license was verified.
notes: Borrowing access is not permission to redistribute the scan.

rights.component: Medieval manuscript text of The Owl and the Nightingale
rights.basis: The underlying medieval text and manuscript writing date from the thirteenth century; this statement does not cover modern photographs or catalog descriptions.
Digital Bodleian attributes image copyright to Jesus College Oxford and offers the images under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0.
notes: The provider says no separate permission is needed when the license conditions and required attribution are followed.

rights.component: Medieval manuscript text of The Owl and the Nightingale
rights.basis: The underlying medieval text and manuscript writing date from the thirteenth century; this statement does not cover modern photographs or provider terms.

rights.jurisdiction: Most countries other than the United Kingdom
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest states: Public Domain in most countries other than the UK.
notes: The geographic qualifier is retained literally rather than generalized to a worldwide public-domain claim.

rights.component: British Library manuscript photographs for UK, repository publication, print-on-demand, or commercial reuse
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place or purpose of reuse
rights.basis: The manifest expressly excludes the UK from its public-domain statement, and current British Library services direct image-reproduction questions to Licensing. No written permission for General Corpus redistribution was verified.
notes: Local caching for research does not itself resolve permission to publish the image bundle or include the images in commercial or print-on-demand outputs.

rights.component: Fein's 2022 print edition and cached complete publisher PDF, including translation, introductions, and notes
rights.basis: The publication preview states Copyright © 2022 by the Board of Trustees of Western Michigan University. METS says print use is governed by the edition's own guidelines and digital editions are for individual use only; the XML license is separate and does not license the PDF.
notes: Free public access does not imply an open redistribution license. Do not republish the cached PDF or extensive editorial content without permission.

rights.component: Rendered METS digital edition and web text
rights.basis: The current METS Copyright & Citation page says digital editions are provided for individual use only and directs requests to reprint the digital text to the Executive Director.

rights.component: METS edition XML, when obtained from the provider
rights.basis: The current METS Copyright & Citation page licenses all XML source code under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0; that license is not extended to the PDF or rendered web edition.
notes: No XML file is cached in this record, and this license must not be applied to the PDF or the manuscript images.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1963 and no item-specific open license or public-domain determination was verified.
The official Zenodo release and included LICENSE distribute DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two independently examined manuscript witnesses with divergent spellings and readings
editorial_practices.representation: Facing, witness-specific texts; departures and emendations signaled, with supplied forms in square brackets
editorial_practices.description: Atkins states that he examined both manuscripts independently, printed each version substantially as it stands, did not normalize the spellings into one text, and marked editorial departures and emendations.
editorial_practices.scope: Complete Cotton and Jesus text streams, their notes, and editorial apparatus

editorial_practices.source_feature: Red Latin opening rubric in Jesus College MS 29
editorial_practices.representation: Uncolored printed heading followed by the note ‘Latin heading in red’
editorial_practices.description: The edition preserves the rubric's wording and explicitly records its red manuscript presentation, but ordinary monochrome print does not reproduce the color as a visual feature.
editorial_practices.scope: Jesus text opening, printed p. 3 (PDF p. 105)

editorial_practices.source_feature: Atkins's complete volume with two witness texts on facing pages and extensive paratext
editorial_practices.representation: Jesus-witness electronic component only; the Cotton text is a separate collateral file and front matter, translation, notes, illustrations, and glossary are omitted
editorial_practices.description: OTA's OwlJ component extracts one witness text from a broader scholarly edition while retaining print-page and manuscript-navigation signals.
editorial_practices.scope: Complete Jesus component within OTA item 1684

editorial_practices.source_feature: Red Latin manuscript rubric printed as a heading in Atkins
editorial_practices.representation: Serialized as the first L element; color and heading status are not retained
editorial_practices.description: The OTA component treats the rubric as line-like text before the 1,794 poem lines, producing 1,795 L tags even though the rubric is not a verse line.
editorial_practices.scope: Opening of the complete OwlJ component

editorial_practices.representation: Jesus-only web text divided into eighteen supplied 100-line navigation groups, with the last group containing 94 poem lines
editorial_practices.description: The U-M item presents the complete Jesus witness text as an edition-derived selection and supplies navigation structure not intrinsic to the medieval witness.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Opening Latin rubric written in red in the Jesus manuscript
editorial_practices.representation: Promoted to a heading before the poem; wording retained but red color not encoded in the text display
editorial_practices.description: The current U-M representation correctly distinguishes the rubric from the poem's 1,794 verse lines while flattening the visual rubrication.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Opening Latin rubric written in red in the Jesus manuscript
editorial_practices.representation: HEAD element before the poem, without a machine-readable color feature
editorial_practices.description: The May 2026 raw XML preserves the heading/verse distinction also present in the pinned CME wrapper, while its reformatted structural wrapper differs from that pinned file.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Jesus College MS 29 base text with corresponding Cotton readings
editorial_practices.representation: Conservative Jesus-based edition corrected and explained through comparison with Cotton
editorial_practices.description: Fein edits the Jesus version as its own witness-specific text and consults Cotton to correct or clarify it without replacing the Jesus base with a conflated critical text.
editorial_practices.scope: The Owl and the Nightingale edited text, translation, and textual notes

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, item 1684, ‘The Owl and the Nightingale.’
evidence.locator: Item metadata, component files, source description, and license
evidence.summary: Current two-component deposit for OwlJ and OwlC; identifies J. W. H. Atkins's 1922 edition with New York publisher Russell & Russell and assigns CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 to the digital item.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive item 1684, owlj-1684.txt, Jesus manuscript component.
evidence.locator: Complete deposited component
evidence.summary: Complete Jesus-witness electronic text with source header, rubric serialized as the first of 1,795 L tags, 1,794 poem lines, print pagination, and legacy manuscript-navigation data.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive item 1684, owlc-1684.txt, collateral Cotton manuscript component.
evidence.locator: Complete deposited component
evidence.summary: Complete collateral Cotton-witness electronic text distributed in the same deposit; it establishes the paired electronic-edition context but is not upstream of the witness-specific OwlJ text.

evidence.citation: Local structural and normalized-text comparison of OTA OwlJ, current MEDictionary OwlJ XML, pinned CME OwlJ XML, and Atkins 1922 scan, 15 July 2026.
evidence.locator: Opening rubric; all 1,794 poetic strings; 47 structured and five literal manuscript locators; source-edition print pp. 105, 115, 117, 121, and 125
evidence.summary: After resolving OTA thorn entities and whitespace, the rubric plus every poetic line matches across the electronic versions. OTA has already misclassified five marginal folio-column labels as line text; U-M, current MEDictionary XML, and the pinned wrapper preserve that anomaly. The source scan shows those strings as marginal navigation, not verse or missing physical text.
notes: The five literal labels occur after poem lines 1236, 1348, 1385, 1422, and 1459 and complete the otherwise structured sequence 229r.1–241v.2.

evidence.citation: J. W. H. Atkins, ed., The Owl and the Nightingale (Cambridge: University Press, 1922), complete Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: Complete 342-page scan; Preface and textual introduction; Jesus odd printed pp. 3–151 (PDF pp. 105–253); Cotton even pp. 2–150
evidence.summary: Confirms editor, publisher, date, complete publication structure, independently examined facing witness texts, bracketed-emendation practice, Latin rubric treatment, and the marginal placement of the five folio-column labels later keyed as line text.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for item owlandnightingal00atkiuoft.
evidence.locator: metadata.possible-copyright-status; metadata.scanningcentre; metadata.contributor; file inventory
evidence.summary: Identifies the 1922 Cambridge volume and its Robarts/University of Toronto scan, exposes complete public derivatives, and expressly records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for region US with scanning evidence of no visible copyright notice.

evidence.citation: Library of Congress, LCCN 77139898, The Owl and the Nightingale, Russell & Russell reprint.
evidence.locator: MARC 008, 245, 260, 300, and 500 fields
evidence.summary: Authoritative record codes the volume as a 1971 reprint of 1922, gives New York: Russell & Russell [1971], xc + 231 pages, states first publication in 1922, and confirms facing Cotton and Jesus texts.

evidence.citation: Open Library, edition OL4577146M, The Owl and the Nightingale (Russell & Russell, 1971).
evidence.locator: Edition metadata
evidence.summary: Secondary discovery record corroborating New York, Russell & Russell, 1971, xc + 231 pages, facing Cotton/Jesus texts, and first publication in 1922.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive controlled-borrow item bwb_T3-BGL-147, The Owl and the Nightingale, Russell & Russell, 1971.
evidence.locator: Item metadata, collections, and file inventory
evidence.summary: Identifies an extant scan of the 1971 Russell & Russell carrier in print-disabled/in-library collections. PDF, OCR, and page-image derivatives are private or LCP-encrypted, so the item is borrowable but not an unrestricted reusable download.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, Jesus College MS 29.
evidence.locator: Part II and item 3, The Owl and the Nightingale
evidence.summary: Describes the composite codex and its trilingual anthology, dates Part II to ?1272 × 1290s, identifies the poem at current ff. 156r–168v, and supplies physical, linguistic, holding, and facsimile information.

evidence.citation: Jesus College Oxford and Digital Bodleian, complete digital facsimile of Jesus College MS 29.
evidence.locator: Complete 532-canvas object; poem at canvases 319–344, current ff. 156ra–168vb
evidence.summary: Complete-codex IIIF facsimile and manifest, including item-level Jesus College attribution and CC BY 4.0 image license; the entire object is cached, not only the poem leaves.

evidence.citation: Local visual inspection of the complete Digital Bodleian Jesus College MS 29 bundle, 15 July 2026.
evidence.locator: Canvases 319–344; opening current f. 156r/old f. 229r
evidence.summary: Verifies the poem's two-column locus, red Latin opening rubric, and the old pencil foliation beginning 229 on current f. 156r, which anchors the legacy locator crosswalk used by Atkins and the electronic encodings.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Caligula A IX.
evidence.locator: The Owl and the Nightingale, ff. 233r–246r; physical description and digital coverage
evidence.summary: Catalogs the collateral Cotton witness, its composite-codex date and south-west-Midlands origin, exact poem locus, satirical verse-debate description, and complete digital coverage.

evidence.citation: British Library, complete IIIF presentation manifest for Cotton MS Caligula A IX.
evidence.locator: Complete 543-canvas object; poem at canvases 475–501, ff. 233r–246r
evidence.summary: Complete-codex facsimile and exact item manifest. The manifest labels the images public domain in most countries other than the UK; the entire object is cached at a practical 1200-pixel profile.

evidence.citation: British Library, Terms and Conditions.
evidence.locator: Websites and Online Services, copyright and public-domain-marked content
evidence.summary: Explains the provider's general framework for public-domain and Creative Commons content, service-specific terms, responsibility for third-party rights, and commercial/database-use restrictions; it does not erase the Cotton manifest's geographic qualifier.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation Services.
evidence.locator: Manuscript-image ordering, reference/reproduction distinction, required shelfmark and folio data, and licensing contact
evidence.summary: Provides mediated ordering and licensing routes for manuscript images, distinguishes reference from reproduction work, and directs intended reproduction or publication enquiries to the provider.

evidence.citation: Susanna Fein, ed. and trans., The Owl and the Nightingale and the English Poems of Oxford, Jesus College, MS 29 (II) (Middle English Texts Series, 2022; digital edition 2024).
evidence.locator: Complete 470-page PDF; edited text and translation at PDF pp. 75–157
evidence.summary: Complete modern Jesus-based edition and translation, with introduction and notes; it corrects conservatively through comparison with Cotton while preserving a witness-specific Jesus base.

evidence.citation: Susanna Fein, ‘The Owl and the Nightingale,’ introduction and edition note in The Owl and the Nightingale and the English Poems of Oxford, Jesus College, MS 29 (II) (2022).
evidence.locator: Article introduction beginning PDF p. 320 and edition note beginning PDF p. 322; General Introduction pp. 16–19 and 40–42
evidence.summary: Current scholarly account of the two surviving witnesses, reconstructed common exemplar, uncertain dating and authorship, Jesus scribal language and adaptation, 1,794-line couplet form, lightly satiric/comic style, major interpretive topics, and deliberately unresolved ending.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright & Citation page.
evidence.locator: Copyright section
evidence.summary: Distinguishes format-specific rights: print is governed by each edition's guidelines; rendered digital editions are for individual use only and require contact with the Executive Director to reprint; XML source code is separately licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

evidence.citation: Publisher preview of Fein 2022, copyright and cataloging page.
evidence.locator: PDF p. 5, copyright and Library of Congress cataloging data
evidence.summary: States Copyright © 2022 by the Board of Trustees of Western Michigan University and confirms the 2022 bibliographic identity, publisher, ISBNs, and print/ebook LCCNs of the edition.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog, record 33407, N. R. Ker, The Owl and the Nightingale: Facsimile of the Jesus and Cotton Manuscripts (EETS o.s. 251, 1963).
evidence.locator: Bibliographic and holdings record
evidence.summary: Confirms the 1963 printed facsimile's title, editor, EETS series number, publisher, physical extent, and coverage of both surviving manuscripts; no complete public digital copy was established.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record 2307.
evidence.locator: dimev-2.0-alpha.1/data/Records.xml, record-2307
evidence.summary: Official prerelease catalog record for the title, incipit, explicit, 1,794 couplet lines, NIMEV 1384, debate/bird subjects, both witness loci, editions, and Ker facsimile references; the archive includes CC BY 4.0 license evidence.
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is explicitly a prerelease; exact Jesus columns are refined from the current catalog and facsimile.

evidence.citation: Parsed Corpus of Middle English Poetry, text record ‘The Owl and the Nightingale.’
evidence.locator: Genre/subjects, original dialect, date, transmission, versification, identifiers, edition, and manuscript-base fields
evidence.summary: Synthesizes the animal-debate genre, weak but broad Southern original localization, unresolved 1175–1285 composition range, 897 couplets and 1,794 lines, common-error transmission, two extant witnesses, and Ker's report of a possible third manuscript now lost.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.locator: Works first published before 1978 and maximum statutory terms
evidence.summary: Provides the U.S. copyright-duration framework used to distinguish the public-domain medieval texts and 1922 edition from modern scans, editions, translations, catalog content, and a 1971 reprint carrier.

open_questions.question: Can the broad 1175–1285 discovery envelope be narrowed among the early, traditional 1189–1216, and later-thirteenth-century/post-1272 proposals?
next_steps: Consult Cartlidge's full dating argument and subsequent scholarship cited by Fein and PCMEP.
Keep manuscript attestation dates separate from the unresolved date of intellectual composition.
Treat the normalized span as a discovery envelope, not a claim of continuous composition or settled endpoints.

open_questions.question: Can the internal references to Nicholas of Guildford support an authorial attribution, or must the work remain anonymous?
next_steps: Review the competing Nicholas-of-Guildford and female-authorship arguments cited by Fein.
Retain Nicholas only as a disputed candidate unless external evidence resolves the internal-reference problem.

open_questions.question: Was OTA item 1684 keyed from a specific 1971 Russell & Russell copy, or does its source line merely combine the 1922 textual edition date with the later reprint publisher?
next_steps: Borrow and inspect the controlled 1971 scan for carrier-specific matter or resetting.
Seek OTA deposit history, production documentation, or a copy-specific source statement.
Compare representative 1971 pages against the public 1922 scan without treating textual agreement alone as carrier proof.

open_questions.question: At what production stage were five marginal folio-column labels misclassified as poem-line text?
next_steps: Recover any older OTA deposit or conversion documentation that predates the current SGML component.
Preserve the verified fact that the anomaly is already present in OTA and survives in later U-M/MED wrappers.
Use the Atkins scan to keep the five strings classified as marginal navigation rather than verse or physical lacunae.

open_questions.question: Can the reported lost manuscript titled ‘De conflictu inter philomenam et bubonem in anglicis’ be identified more securely or placed in relation to the reconstructed exemplar X?
next_steps: Inspect Ker 1963, introduction p. ix n. 4, and the catalog or inventory source underlying his notice.
Do not merge the reported lost item with X unless physical or textual evidence establishes that identity.

open_questions.question: Is there a lawful complete public digital copy of Ker's 1963 EETS facsimile?
next_steps: Check EETS and additional holding libraries for a provider-authorized digital surrogate.
Use the complete current institutional manuscript facsimiles for visual research in the meantime.
notes: This date-bounded negative does not prove that no private, subscription, or unindexed copy exists.

open_questions.question: What permission or provider terms apply to UK, repository-publication, print-on-demand, or commercial reuse of the British Library Cotton facsimile images?
next_steps: Obtain a written determination from British Library Licensing for the intended jurisdiction and publication context.
Retain the exact item-level statement ‘Public Domain in most countries other than the UK’ without generalizing it worldwide.
Keep local research caching separate from authorization to redistribute the image bundle.

open_questions.question: Do the current U-M/MED no-copyright and unrestricted-reuse statements, or the Kyvero-Press/CME root Unlicense, resolve or supersede the pinned OwlJ artifact's retained legacy personal-scholarly-use-only notice for redistribution of this exact XML?
next_steps: Request written clarification from the University of Michigan Library/Middle English Compendium about redistribution and commercial reuse of the legacy-derived raw OwlJ encoding, citing the exact pinned file and current download statement.
Ask the Kyvero-Press/CME maintainer whether the repository Unlicense was intended to cover inherited corpus XML data and retained third-party notices.

Until clarified, keep the pinned repository artifact, current U-M presentation, current MED XML, OTA deposit, scholarly editions, and manuscript-image rights as separate layers and avoid treating one layer's statement as blanket permission for all others.
